quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium"?

options.text: The first photosystem in the light reactions; absorbs 680nm light, splits water, and passes excited electrons to the electron transport chain
A mathematical model predicting allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations in the absence of evolutionary forces
The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix
A mathematical model estimating a population's probability of persistence over a specified time period, incorporating demographic and environmental variability

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium" is defined as: A mathematical model predicting allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations in the absence of evolutionary forces. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Allele frequency"?

options.text: The proportion of a specific allele in a population: p = frequency of dominant allele, q = frequency of recessive allele; p + q = 1
A gene-editing system using guide RNA to direct the Cas9 nuclease to cut DNA at a precise location, enabling precise gene knockout, correction, or insertion
Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with favorable heritable traits in a given environment; the primary mechanism of adaptive evolution
The variety of life at three levels: genetic diversity (within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance), and ecosystem diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Allele frequency" is defined as: The proportion of a specific allele in a population: p = frequency of dominant allele, q = frequency of recessive allele; p + q = 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Genotype frequency"?

options.text: A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
A structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls made of β-glucose linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds; forms rigid, indigestible fibers
Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate
The proportion of each genotype in a population: p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1 under Hardy-Weinberg conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Genotype frequency" is defined as: The proportion of each genotype in a population: p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1 under Hardy-Weinberg conditions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hardy-Weinberg conditions"?

options.text: A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids
Small intracellular molecules (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) that relay and amplify signals from the receptor to target proteins
No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hardy-Weinberg conditions" is defined as: No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Carrier frequency"?

options.text: The molecular machine for translation; composed of large and small subunits containing rRNA and proteins; has A, P, and E sites
2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)
A signaling molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor) that binds specifically to a receptor protein
A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that controls what enters and exits the cell; selectively permeable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Carrier frequency" is defined as: 2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A mathematical model predicting allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations in the absence of evolutionary forces"?

options.text: Law of Segregation
Dominant and recessive
Exponential growth
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The proportion of a specific allele in a population: p = frequency of dominant allele, q = frequency of recessive allele; p + q = 1"?

options.text: r-selected species
Recombinant DNA
Density-dependent inhibition
Allele frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Allele frequency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The proportion of each genotype in a population: p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1 under Hardy-Weinberg conditions"?

options.text: Genotype frequency
Final electron acceptor
Crossing over
Phospholipid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Genotype frequency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution"?

options.text: Angiogenesis
Final electron acceptor
Recombination frequency
Hardy-Weinberg conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hardy-Weinberg conditions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)"?

options.text: Carrier frequency
Species-area relationship
Biodiversity hotspot
Endomembrane system

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Carrier frequency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Population Genetics is accurate?

options.text: Community Ecology examines interactions between species in ecological communities.
Sexual selection and artificial selection are special cases of selection that shape traits beyond survival.
The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.
Population Genetics provides the mathematical framework for studying evolution using allele and genotype frequencies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Population Genetics?

options.text: The Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (p² + 2pq + q² = 1, p + q = 1) serves as a null hypothesis: populations are in equilibrium only when there is no mutation, migration, selection, drift, or non-random mating.
Regulation of Enzyme Activity controls metabolic pathways through allosteric regulation, competitive and noncompetitive inhibition, and feedback inhibition.

Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added one by one as ribosome moves along mRNA), and termination (release factor binds stop codon).
Logistic growth (dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K) incorporates carrying capacity K and produces S-shaped curves.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Population Genetics?

options.text: Deviations from H-W equilibrium indicate evolution is occurring.
The specific bond orientation (α vs. β linkages) determines digestibility and function: α-1,4 bonds in starch are digestible by humans, while β-1,4 bonds in cellulose are not.
The three modes of natural selection are directional (shifts the mean), stabilizing (reduces variation around the mean), and disruptive (favors extremes).
Properties of Water make it essential for life due to its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Population Genetics?

options.text: Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
Genomic technologies (DNA sequencing, gene editing with CRISPR-Cas9, and bioinformatics) have revolutionized biology, medicine, agriculture, and forensics.
Calculating allele frequencies from phenotype data allows estimation of carrier frequencies for genetic diseases.
Cell Structure covers the organization of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Population Genetics?

options.text: Biodiversity and Conservation addresses the variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels and the threats to it.
Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) activated by cyclins drive progression past checkpoints.
Photosystems II and I absorb photons, exciting electrons that pass through electron transport chains.
Population Genetics provides the mathematical framework for studying evolution using allele and genotype frequencies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Population Genetics?

options.text: Estimating cystic fibrosis carrier frequency: disease frequency q² ≈ 1/2500, so q ≈ 1/50, and carrier frequency 2pq ≈ 1/25 in European populations
Wildlife management using logistic growth models to calculate maximum sustainable yield for harvested populations (fisheries, deer management)
Hemophilia in European royal families: X-linked recessive inheritance traced through pedigrees from Queen Victoria to multiple royal houses
Captive breeding and reintroduction programs recovering the California condor from 22 individuals (1982) to over 500, including 300+ in the wild

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Population Genetics?

options.text: Biofuel production engineering algae with enhanced Calvin cycle efficiency to convert CO₂ into lipids for biodiesel
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years
Cancer cell organelle abnormalities: enlarged nuclei, increased rough ER (elevated protein synthesis), and altered mitochondrial metabolism (Warburg effect)
Detecting natural selection by comparing observed genotype frequencies to Hardy-Weinberg predictions and identifying significant deviations using chi-square tests

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Population Genetics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Metabolic syndrome and diabetes: dysregulation of glucose metabolism affecting cellular respiration, insulin signaling, and energy storage pathways
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years
Conservation biology using Hardy-Weinberg analysis to assess genetic health of endangered species populations and plan genetic rescue interventions
The Human Genome Project using genetic and physical mapping to determine the positions of all ~20,000 human genes across 23 chromosome pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Population Genetics?

options.text: Estimating cystic fibrosis carrier frequency: disease frequency q² ≈ 1/2500, so q ≈ 1/50, and carrier frequency 2pq ≈ 1/25 in European populations
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years

Wolf reintroduction into Yellowstone (1995) demonstrating trophic cascades: wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing riparian vegetation recovery, which stabilized stream banks and increased beaver populations
HPV vaccines (Gardasil) preventing cervical cancer by protecting against viral proteins (E6, E7) that inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and Rb

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Population Genetics in practice?

options.text: Amazon deforestation averaging ~10,000 km² per year, threatening the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem with an estimated 10% of all species on Earth
Exercise physiology: the Krebs cycle's rate increases during aerobic exercise as increased ATP consumption lowers ATP/ADP ratio, relieving allosteric inhibition
Detecting natural selection by comparing observed genotype frequencies to Hardy-Weinberg predictions and identifying significant deviations using chi-square tests
Xeroderma pigmentosum caused by defective nucleotide excision repair, making patients extremely sensitive to UV radiation-induced DNA damage

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Genetics.
